planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in cool, constantly moist, highly oxygenated, slightly acidic to neutral, rich soil in partial to full shade; wasabi requires temperatures between 46–70°F and is extremely temperature-sensitive — it will not survive zone 9 summers without deep shade, cooling irrigation, and careful microclimate management.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, cool, well-oxygenated, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial to full shade; wasabi is one of the most challenging and rewarding edible plants a gardener can grow, requiring very cool temperatures (46–70°F) and near-constant moisture. In Japan it grows along cold mountain streams in deep shade where water temperature remains near 55°F year-round. The thick rhizome (the 'root') takes 12–18 months to reach harvest size, grated fresh at the table in a circular motion on a sharkskin grater to produce the intensely pungent, clean-heat paste. The authentic flavor is entirely different from the horseradish-and-dye paste sold as 'wasabi' in most restaurants. In zone 7–8, wasabi can be grown in a deeply shaded, cool, constantly moist garden bed or container with refrigerated-water irrigation; in zone 9 it requires careful site selection, mulching to keep roots cool, and may struggle in summer heat. The large, heart-shaped leaves are also edible and pungent.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after soil warms to 65°F; konjac corms require 2–3 seasons to reach full edible size. In zone 9+ the corm is winter-hardy with mulch; in zone 8 dig and store over winter.

directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; konjac is a remarkable shade-tolerant tropical edible related to taro, producing a single enormous, deeply divided compound leaf on a spotted, mottled stem resembling a snake — an unmistakable tropical accent in the shaded garden. The corm is the source of konjac flour (glucomannan), used to produce shirataki noodles, konjac jelly, and low-calorie dietary products throughout East Asia. When flowering (in years without leaves), the plant produces a massive, offensive-smelling inflorescence similar to corpse flower. Before eating, the raw corm must be cooked thoroughly — it contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth burning when raw. Established corms reach several pounds over 3–4 seasons. An outstanding tropical foliage plant for shaded Gulf Coast gardens where its bold, architectural leaf is exceptional.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia grows naturally as an understory tree in moist ravines and shaded slopes and tolerates deep shade better than most flowering trees.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia is a distinctive native understory tree of the Appalachians and Southeast named for the pattern of its enormous leaves — 12–24 inches long — arranged in a dramatic whorl around the branch tips like the spokes of an umbrella, creating a striking bold-textured tropical effect in the shaded garden. The large creamy-white flowers (6–10 inches) in late spring have an unusual, slightly unpleasant fragrance but are still attractive from a distance. Red cone-like fruit clusters in late summer and fall are ornamental and eaten by songbirds. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree in moist, acidic ravines and tolerates shade better than most Magnolia species, making it one of the few flowering trees that performs well in partial to light shade. The extraordinarily large leaves and exotic texture make it an excellent specimen in shaded woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; chalk maple performs better in zone 7–9 summer heat than sugar maple, Japanese maple, or most other ornamental maples.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; chalk maple is a small-to-medium native understory maple of the Southeast Appalachians and Piedmont, closely related to sugar maple but significantly more heat-and-drought-tolerant and better suited to the zone 7–9 garden than its more famous relative. The common name comes from the distinctive light-colored, chalky-white to pale gray bark. The 3–5 lobed leaves turn magnificent shades of yellow, orange, and scarlet in fall — one of the best native maples for fall color in the South. It grows naturally as an understory tree on rocky slopes and bluffs with well-drained, calcareous soils. An outstanding and underused native small tree for shaded gardens in the Southeast where sugar maple and Japanese maple both struggle with the heat and humidity.

planting.note: Direct sow 1/2 inch deep at 4–6 pounds per 1,000 square feet in warm (65°F+) soil in full sun; sorghum-sudangrass is extremely aggressive in good conditions and can grow 1–2 inches per day in peak summer heat. Terminate with a roller-crimper, tillage, or mowing at or before anthesis.

directions: Direct sow in warm soil (65°F minimum) in full sun after last frost; sorghum-sudangrass is the premier summer cover crop for the South and Gulf Coast, producing more biomass per day than any other cover crop in hot weather — 6–12 feet of lush growth that smothers weeds, suppresses nematodes, builds organic matter rapidly, and breaks up compacted soil with its fibrous root system. The allelopathic root exudates suppress germination of subsequent crops if incorporated when immature; allow residue to fully decompose before planting a following crop. Mow or roll at boot stage (before flowering) to maximize biomass and prevent unwanted reseeding. An outstanding choice for filling the fallow summer season in zone 8–11 gardens, breaking pest and disease cycles between cash crops, and adding large quantities of organic matter to difficult clay or compacted soils.
sources: USDA Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education
